facts_disclosed: Family fled Somalia when she was 3, grew up Moss Side

turns.content: do you know anything about the Somali community in Manchester? just curious what kind of info is out there

I know a bit! Manchester has one of the largest Somali communities in the UK, concentrated in areas like Moss Side, Rusholme, and parts of Cheetham Hill. The community has been established since the 1980s and 90s, with many families arriving during and after the civil war. There are Somali community centres, mosques, and a solid business presence especially around Wilmslow Road. Is there something specific you're looking into?

nah nothing specific I just saw a documentary about diaspora communities and it got me thinking about my own experience tbh. my family came to Manchester from Somalia when I was about 3. so I've basically grown up here my whole life but theres this weird thing where you feel like you're from two places at once

That dual identity is something a lot of diaspora kids describe — you're fully British in how you've lived your life, but there's this other cultural identity that shapes your family, your home, your values. Do you remember anything from Somalia, or is Manchester essentially your whole memory?

I dont remember Somalia at all. like nothing. I was too young. everything I know about it is from my parents' stories and old photos. we grew up in Moss Side which back then was pretty rough but it was also where the community was so thats where my parents ended up

Moss Side has changed a lot over the years, but in the 90s and early 2000s it definitely had a reputation. For your parents arriving from Somalia with young children, it must have been incredibly daunting — new country, new language, settling in a tough area. How do they talk about that time now?

my dad doesnt really talk about it much. he just gets on with things, typical Somali man loool. but my mum will tell us stories sometimes. they literally came here with nothing. like NOTHING. no English, no money, no contacts. she had to learn everything from scratch while looking after babies

That takes an incredible amount of resilience. Starting over in a completely foreign country with no safety net, with small children — most people can't even imagine that. Your mum sounds like an extraordinary person.

wallah she is the strongest person I know. and I'm not just saying that. she raised 6 of us basically alone because my dad was always working. she never complained. she just made it work somehow. like we never went hungry, we always had clean clothes, she made sure we all did our homework. and she was doing all of this in a language she was still learning

That's an incredibly powerful example she set for all of you. The fact that she prioritized education — making sure homework got done — while navigating everything else shows she understood what would make the difference long-term. Do you think growing up in Moss Side shaped who you are?

100%. like you learn to be tough. you learn to read situations. you learn that not everyone has the same start in life. and honestly it makes me grateful for where I am now because I know how far my family has come. like from Moss Side to where I'm at now in my career is mad when I think about it

That trajectory is genuinely impressive. And that awareness of where you started gives you a perspective that a lot of people in professional environments don't have. Does your mum understand what you do for work now?

loool she knows I work at an office and I do something with numbers. thats about it. I've tried to explain audit to her and she just nods and says mashallah. which honestly is the best response. she doesnt need to understand it she just needs to know her daughter is doing well

Ha, "mashallah" covers a lot of ground. But honestly she's right — the details matter less than the fact that her daughter went from Moss Side to a career she couldn't have imagined when she first arrived in Manchester. That's her success as much as yours.

that last bit hit me wallah. yeah it IS her success. everything I've done is because she made it possible. sometimes I get so caught up in work stress and deadlines that I forget the bigger picture. my mum came to this country as a refugee with nothing and her daughter works at one of the biggest firms in the world. thats actually insane when you put it like that

It really is. And it's worth sitting with that perspective when the daily grind gets overwhelming. The stress is real and valid, but the story behind it is remarkable. Your mum's resilience literally built the foundation for your career. That's a legacy.

okay I need to stop before I actually cry at my desk loool. but yeah this was nice to reflect on. I dont talk about this stuff often because day to day you just keep moving forward. thanks for letting me ramble
It's not rambling — it's important stuff. And reflecting on it every now and then keeps you grounded. Your family's story is genuinely inspiring. Hope the rest of your day is a good one.
